papers.title: Supernovae in the Central Parsec: A Mechanism for Producing Spatially Anisotropic Hypervelocity Stars
papers.author_names: Kastytis Zubovas Graham A. Wynn Alessia Gualandris

abstract.text: Several tens of hyper-velocity stars (HVSs) have been discovered escaping our Galaxy. These stars share a common origin in the Galactic centre and are distributed anisotropically in Galactic longitude and latitude. We examine the possibility that HVSs may be created as the result of supernovae occurring within binary systems in a disc of stars around Sgr A* over the last 100 Myr. Monte Carlo simulations show that the rate of binary disruption is ~10^-4 yr^-1, comparable to that of tidal disruption models. The supernova-induced HVS production rate (\\\\Gamma_HVS) is significantly increased if the binaries are hardened via migration through a gaseous disc. Moderate hardening gives \\\\Gamma_HVS ~ 2*10^-7 yr^-1 and an estimated population of ~20 HVSs in the last 100 Myr. Supernova-induced HVS production requires the internal and external orbital velocity vectors of the secondary binary component to be aligned when the binary is disrupted. This leaves an imprint of the disc geometry on the spatial distribution of the HVSs, producing a distinct anisotropy.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A mechanism for producing hyper-velocity stars (HVSs) via supernova-induced disruption of binary systems in a stellar disc surrounding Sgr A* is proposed. Monte Carlo simulations show that binary disruption rates reach ∼10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹, with moderate orbital hardening via gaseous disc migration increasing the HVS production rate to ∼2×10⁻⁷ yr⁻¹ and yielding ∼20 HVSs over 100 Myr. The model predicts spatial anisotropy in HVS distribution due to alignment of secondary orbital and internal velocity vectors, imprinting the disc's geometry and matching observed HVS arrival times and Galactic center origin.

source_fields.title: Supernovae in the Central Parsec: A Mechanism for Producing Spatially Anisotropic Hypervelocity Stars

source_fields.sections_text: Results: Supernovae in massive binaries disrupt nearly 93–96% of binaries, producing a fiducial disruption rate of $\\sim 10^{-4} \\mathrm{yr}^{-1}$ and ejecting $\\sim 4\\%$ to $25\\%$ of secondary stars depending on binary hardening, with a stellar ejection rate of $\\sim 8 \\times 10^{-6} \\mathrm{yr}^{-1}$ and a significant boost in high-velocity star production ($>275\\,\\mathrm{km/s}$) at orbital radii of $0.1-0.5\\,\\mathrm{pc}$. Discussion: Supernova-induced disruption of binary stars in the Galactic center can eject stars at rates sufficient to explain most observed hypervelocity stars (HVSs), with binary hardening occurring on timescales of ~10⁵ years via disc migration, leading to ejection efficiencies that are consistent with observations and producing spatially anisotropic stellar ejection directions. Conclusions: Monte Carlo simulations show that core-collapse supernovae in binary systems near Sgr A* produce HVSs at rates comparable to tidal disruption, with stronger binary hardening increasing HVS yields but reducing spatial anisotropy, suggesting that binaries with initially smaller separations in the Galactic center are needed to explain the observed HVS population. Introduction: This paper proposes a novel mechanism for hypervelocity star production via supernova-induced binary disruption in a Galactic Centre stellar disc, demonstrating that this model reproduces observed HVS velocities, anisotropic distributions, and arrival times consistent with a GC origin while offering an alternative to tidal disruption models. Hvs Production Mechanisms: The primary mechanisms for producing hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are tidal disruption of stellar binaries by supermassive black holes at the Galactic Center and dynamical ejection from the Galactic disk via binary supernovae or stellar encounters, with the former explaining most Galactic Center-origin HVSs and the latter accounting for disk-based runaways and hyper-runaways. Supernovae In Galactic Centre Binaries: This section models supernova-driven hypervelocity star (HVS) production in Galactic center binaries, showing that HVS formation depends on the alignment of natal kicks, internal binary orbits, and external galactic orbits, with the resulting anisotropic HVS distribution potentially reflecting the orientation of a fossil stellar disc in the GC ~100 Myr ago.

abstract: Several tens of hyper-velocity stars (HVSs) have been discovered escaping our Galaxy.
These stars share a common origin in the Galactic centre and are distributed anisotropically in Galactic longitude and latitude.
We examine the possibility that HVSs may be created as the result of supernovae occurring within binary systems in a disc of stars around Sgr A* over the last 100 Myr.
title: Supernovae in the Central Parsec: A Mechanism for Producing Spatially Anisotropic Hypervelocity Stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical mechanism paper using Monte Carlo simulations to model HVS production via supernova-induced binary disruption. No observational data or catalog presented.

papers.title: On the origin of the B-stars in the Galactic center
papers.author_names: Ann-Marie Madigan Oliver Pfuhl Yuri Levin Stefan Gillessen Reinhard Genzel Hagai B. Perets

abstract.text: We present a new directly-observable statistic which uses sky position and proper motion of stars near the Galactic center massive black hole to identify populations with high orbital eccentricities. It is most useful for stars with large orbital periods for which dynamical accelerations are difficult to determine. We apply this statistic to a data set of B-stars with projected radii 0."1 < p < 25" (~0.004 - 1 pc) from the massive black hole in the Galactic center. We compare the results with those from N-body simulations to distinguish between scenarios for their formation. We find that the scenarios favored by the data correlate strongly with particular K-magnitude intervals, corresponding to different zero-age main-sequence (MS) masses and lifetimes. Stars with 14 < mK < 15 (15 - 20 solar masses, t_{MS} = 8-13 Myr) match well to a disk formation origin, while those with mK > 15 (<15 solar masses, t_{MS} >13 Myr), if isotropically distributed, form a population that is more eccentric than thermal, which suggests a Hills binary-disruption origin.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: galactic-center dynamical origins LLM confirmed: Abstract discusses B-stars with high orbital eccentricities and suggests a Hills binary-disruption origin, which is a direct mechanism for producing high-velocity/runaway stars.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: 6. Discussion: The $h$-statistic reveals that the binary disruption scenario produces a radial decrease in mean $|h|$ and scatter, consistent with high eccentricities in B-stars, while the dissolved disk scenario yields broader $|h|$ distributions dependent on viewing angle, and S-stars exhibit slightly higher eccentricities than thermal predictions, with the observed $|h|$ trends in B-stars indicating greater eccentricity in lower-mass, older stars regardless of azimuthal sampling. 1. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel kinematic analysis of early-type stars in the Galactic center's nuclear star cluster to distinguish between in-situ disk formation and cluster merger scenarios, addressing the problem of constraining the origin of stellar populations that underlie the growth of the central supermassive black hole. 4. Observational data: This section describes the observational data and data reduction methods used to identify early-type stars near SgrA*, combining adaptive optics imaging and spectroscopy to distinguish young, massive stars from old giants despite extreme crowding and extinction. 3. $N$-body simulations: We perform $N$-body simulations to investigate whether the orbital eccentricities of large-radii B-stars in the Galactic Center are preserved over 6 Myr under different formation scenarios, accounting for gravitational torquing from the young stellar disk and including both Newtonian and general relativistic precession effects. Appendix D Convergence Testing: Convergence testing shows that binary disruption simulations with different stellar masses (20M⊙ and 50M⊙) produce statistically indistinguishable results in terms of the ℎ-statistic, regardless of viewing direction. 2. The high-eccentricity statistic: A new statistic, $h$, derived by normalizing the angular momentum along the line-of-sight to the circular velocity at projected radius, provides a more sensitive and accurate identification of high-eccentricity stellar orbits compared to the traditional $j$ statistic. 5. Comparison between observations and simulations: The observational data, particularly for lower-magnitude B-stars, better match the dissolved disk formation scenario in terms of $|h|$-values and radial distributions, while higher-magnitude stars align more closely with the binary disruption scenario, and completeness corrections and observational errors confirm the preference for the dissolved disk model. Appendix A Constraints on $h$-statistic for bound stars: The $h$-statistic for bound stellar orbits is constrained by the escape velocity, leading to $h \\leq \\sqrt{2}$, with values $1 < |h| \\leq \\sqrt{2}$ indicating the star is near periapsis and providing additional constraints on its orbital parameters and line-of-sight position. Appendix B Effect of massive stellar potential on value of $h$-statistic: The presence of a stellar cusp increases the apparent value of the $h$-statistic for orbits due to the additional mass contributing to angular momentum, leading to an overestimation of orbital eccentricity and a systematic bias that depends on the stellar density profile and radius. Appendix C Statistical constraints on orbital eccentricity and inclination from the $h$-statistic: This section derives statistical constraints on stellar orbital eccentricity from the $h$-statistic by fitting cumulative eccentricity distributions to a Gaussian-error function with trigonometric corrections, revealing that higher $|h|$ values correlate with lower eccentricities and enabling inference of eccentricity distributions under isotropic thermal assumptions.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Applies new statistical method to existing dataset of B-stars near Galactic center with proper motions and positions to identify high-eccentricity populations, suggesting binary-disruption origin.
